elements.sent-2411-001: The urban environment is characterized by complex spatio-temporal dynamics arising from diverse human activities and interactions.
elements.sent-2411-002: Effectively modeling these dynamics is essential for understanding and optimizing urban systems.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this work, we introduce UrbanDiT, a foundation model for open-world urban spatio-temporal learning that successfully scale up diffusion transformers in this field.
elements.sent-2411-004: UrbanDiT pioneers a unified model that integrates diverse spatio-temporal data sources and types while learning universal spatio-temporal patterns across different cities and scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-005: This allows the model to unify both multi-data and multi-task learning, and effectively support a wide range of spatio-temporal applications.
elements.sent-2411-006: Its key innovation lies in the elaborated prompt learning framework, which adaptively generates both data-driven and task-specific prompts, guiding the model to deliver superior performance across various urban applications.
elements.sent-2411-007: UrbanDiT offers three primary advantages: 1) It unifies diverse data types, such as grid-based and graph-based data, into a sequential format, allowing to capture spatio-temporal dynamics across diverse scenarios of different cities; 2) With masking strategies and task-specific prompts, it supports a wide range of tasks, including bi-directional spatio-temporal prediction, temporal interpolation, spatial extrapolation, and spatio-temporal imputation; and 3) It generalizes effectively to open-world scenarios, with its powerful zero-shot capabilities outperforming nearly all baselines with training data.
elements.sent-2411-008: These features allow UrbanDiT to achieves state-of-the-art performance in different domains such as transportation traffic, crowd flows, taxi demand, bike usage, and cellular traffic, across multiple cities and tasks.
elements.sent-2411-009: UrbanDiT sets up a new benchmark for foundation models in the urban spatio-temporal domain.
elements.sent-2411-010: Code and datasets are publicly available at \url{https://github.com/YuanYuan98/UrbanDiT .}

It is a foundation model that integrates diverse data sources and types while simultaneously performing multiple tasks.} \label{fig:intro}
elements.sent-2411-011: The urban environment is characterized by complex spatio-temporal dynamics arising from diverse human activities and interactions within the city.
elements.sent-2411-012: These dynamics are reflected in different types of data.
elements.sent-2411-013: For example, grid-based data divides urban space into regular cells, often used to track crowd flows.
elements.sent-2411-014: In contrast, graph-based data represents spatial structures like road networks as nodes and edges, such as traffic speeds on roads.
elements.sent-2411-015: These data sources usually come from different cities, each with unique layouts, infrastructures, and planning strategies.
elements.sent-2411-016: Effectively modeling these diverse spatio-temporal dynamics is crucial for optimizing urban services and understanding how cities function and evolve.
elements.sent-2411-017: Therefore, it raises an essential research question: can we develop a foundation model, similar to those in natural language processing~\citep{touvron2023llama,brown2020language and computer vision~\citep{brooks2024video,liu2023instaflow, esser2024scaling , that learns universal spatio-temporal patterns and serves as a general-purpose model for various urban applications?.}}

elements.sent-2411-020: By training on multiple datasets, they have showcased impressive generalization capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-021: However, their focus remains largely on prediction tasks, and they are often restricted to specific data types—such as grid-based data~\citep{li2024urbangpt, yuan2024unist or graph-based traffic data~\citep{yuan2024spatio .}}
elements.sent-2411-022: Thus, realizing the full potential of foundation models capable of seamlessly handling diverse data types, sources, and tasks in open-world scenarios remains an open and largely unexplored area of research.
elements.sent-2411-023: Urban spatio-temporal data is typically defined by diverse properties, including varying spatial resolutions, temporal dynamics, and complex interactions among entities.
elements.sent-2411-024: Building an effective foundation model requires a scalable architecture capable of accommodating these complexities.
elements.sent-2411-025: Moreover, the intricate nature of urban spatio-temporal dynamics necessitates a model that can learn from complex data distributions.
elements.sent-2411-026: Diffusion Transformers, exemplified by models like Sora~\citep{brooks2024video , offer a compelling solution for this purpose.}
elements.sent-2411-027: By combining the generative power of diffusion processes with the scalability and flexibility of transformer architectures, diffusion transformers present a promising backbone.
elements.sent-2411-028: In this work, we introduce UrbanDiT, which unifies training across diverse urban scenarios and tasks, effectively scaling up diffusion transformers for comprehensive urban spatio-temporal learning.
elements.sent-2411-029: It offers three appealing benefits: 1) It unifies diverse data types into a sequential format, allowing it to capture spatio-temporal patterns across various cities and domains, guided by data-driven prompts that highlight critical patterns.
elements.sent-2411-030: To achieve this, we first unify different input data by converting it into the sequential format.
elements.sent-2411-031: We build the denoising network using transformer blocks, equipped with both temporal and spatial attention modules.
elements.sent-2411-032: To integrate diverse data types and tasks, we propose a unified prompt learning framework that enhances the denoising process.
elements.sent-2411-033: This framework maintains memory pools to capture learned spatio-temporal patterns and generate data-driven prompts, while also create task-specific prompts for various spatio-temporal tasks.
elements.sent-2411-034: These prompts are concatenated into the unified sequential input before being fed into the transformer modules.
elements.sent-2411-035: The design of prompt learning serves as a flexible intermediary, adaptable to a wide range of scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-036: UrbanDiT, built on the DiT backbone with a prompt learning framework, is a pioneering open-world foundation model.
elements.sent-2411-037: It excels at handling diverse urban spatio-temporal data and a wide range of tasks, including bi-directional spatio-temporal prediction, temporal interpolation, spatial extrapolation, and spatio-temporal imputation.
elements.sent-2411-038: This makes UrbanDiT a powerful and universal solution for various urban spatio-temporal applications.
elements.sent-2411-039: We summarize our contributions as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-040: To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to explore a foundation model for general-purpose urban spatio-temporal learning, integrating diverse spatio-temporal data types and multiple urabn tasks within a single unified model.
elements.sent-2411-041: We present UrbanDiT, an open-world foundation model built on diffusion transformers.
elements.sent-2411-042: Through our proposed prompt learning, UrbanDiT effectively brings together heterogeneous spatio-temporal data and tasks, using data-driven and task-specific prompts to enhance performance.
elements.sent-2411-043: Extensive experiments demonstrate that UrbanDiT effectively captures complex urban spatio-temporal dynamics, achieving state-of-the-art performance across multiple datasets and tasks.
elements.sent-2411-044: It also exhibits powerful zero-shot capabilities, proving its applicability in open-world settings.
elements.sent-2411-045: UrbanDiT marks a significant step forward in the advancement of urban foundation models.
elements.sent-2411-046: Urban spatio-temporal learning encompass a variety of tasks such as prediction~\citep{tan2023openstl,bai2020adaptive,yuan2023spatio,li2018diffusion,zhang2017deep , interpolation~\citep{aumond2018kriging,graler2016spatio , extrapolation~\citep{miller2004spatial,ma2019temporal , and imputation~\citep{tashiro2021csdi,hu2023towards , addressing how urban systems evolve across space and time.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: Deep learning has achieved significant progress in these areas, with techniques ranging from CNNs~\citep{li2018diffusion,zhang2017deep , RNNs~\citep{wang2017predrnn, wang2018predrnn++,lin2020self , MLPs~\citep{shao2022spatial , GNNs~\citep{bai2020adaptive,geng2019spatiotemporal , and Transformers~\citep{chen2022bidirectional,jiang2023pdformer , to the more recent use of diffusion models~\citep{yuan2023spatio,yuan2024spatio,tashiro2021csdi,wen2023diffstg .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: Each of these approaches has been employed to model complicated spatio-temporal relationships inherent to urban environments.
elements.sent-2411-049: However, most existing models are tailored to specific datasets and tasks.
elements.sent-2411-050: In contrast, our approach is designed to handle multiple tasks and generalize across diverse urban scenarios without the need for re-training on new datasets.
elements.sent-2411-051: Foundation models have made significant progress in language models~\citep{touvron2023llama,brown2020language and image generation~\citep{brooks2024video,liu2023instaflow, esser2024scaling .}}
elements.sent-2411-052: Recently, researchers have extended the concept of foundation models to urban environments, aiming to address unique challenges of urban spatio-temporal data.
elements.sent-2411-053: Some representative works in this area include UrbanGPT~\citep{li2024urbangpt , UniST~\citep{yuan2024unist , and CityGPT~\citep{feng2024citygpt .}}}
elements.sent-2411-054: UrbanGPT introduces LLMs designed for spatio-temporal predictions within urban contexts.
elements.sent-2411-055: UniST develops a foundation model from scratch specifically for urban prediction tasks, demonstrating zero-shot capabilities that allow the model to generalize to new scenarios without additional training.
elements.sent-2411-056: CityGPT, on the other hand, focuses on enhancing the LLM’s ability to comprehend and solve urban tasks by improving its understanding of urban spaces.
elements.sent-2411-057: Table~\ref{tbl:compare provides a comparison of key abilities across existing urban foundation models and UrbanDiT.}
elements.sent-2411-058: As shown, UrbanDiT is trained from scratch, allowing it to fully leverage data diversity while offering flexibility across a wide range of tasks.
elements.sent-2411-059: Additionally, it demonstrates emergent zero-shot capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-060: Compared to previous efforts, UrbanDiT represents a significant advancement in developing urban foundation models.
elements.sent-2411-061: Diffusion models, originally popularized in image generation, have recently gained attention in handling spatio-temporal data and time series.
elements.sent-2411-062: They iteratively add and remove noise from data, allowing them to capture complex patterns across both temporal and spatial dimensions~\citep{yang2024survey,yuan2023spatio,hu2023towards, wen2023diffstg,rasul2021autoregressive .}
elements.sent-2411-063: In the context of time series, diffusion models have been applied to tasks such as forecasting~\citep{kollovieh2024predict, rasul2021autoregressive and imputation~\citep{xiao2023imputation, tashiro2021csdi , outperforming traditional methods by generating more accurate and coherent sequences.}}
elements.sent-2411-064: For spatio-temporal data, diffusion models have proven useful in a variety of tasks, including traffic prediction~\citep{wen2023diffstg , environmental monitoring~\citep{yuan2023spatio , and human mobility generation~\citep{zhu2024controltraj, zhu2023difftraj .}}}
elements.sent-2411-065: By effectively modeling spatio-temporal dependencies, these models can capture both the spatial correlations and temporal dynamics inherent in urban systems.
elements.sent-2411-066: UrbanDiT leverages the generative power of diffusion models to capture complex urban spatio-temporal patterns, while its flexible conditioning mechanisms allow it to address a wide range of spatio-temporal tasks.
elements.sent-2411-067: This makes UrbanDiT a significant advancement in applying diffusion models to urban spatio-temporal challenges.
elements.sent-2411-069: Urban spatio-temporal data typically falls into two categories: grid-based and graph-based data.
elements.sent-2411-070: Grid-based data is structured in a uniform grid layout.
elements.sent-2411-071: Graph-based data, on the other hand, highlights connectivity, capturing the relationships between various urban entities like streets and intersections.
elements.sent-2411-072: For both different spatial organizations, the temporal dimension is characterized as time series data.
elements.sent-2411-073: The data can be denoted as $X^{N\times T}$, where $N$ denotes the number of spatial partitions.
elements.sent-2411-074: For graph-based data, $N$ corresponds to the number of nodes, while for grid-based data, it is defined as the product of the height and width of the grid ($N=H\times W$).
elements.sent-2411-075: This enables a unified representation of urban spatio-temporal data with different spatial organizations.. Urban Spatio-Temporal Tasks.
elements.sent-2411-076: In addition to the commonly recognized (1) forward prediction task, urban spatio-temporal analysis encompasses several other critical tasks.
elements.sent-2411-077: It is essential for understanding historical trends and validating predictive models.
elements.sent-2411-078: It is important for assessing potential changes in urban environments and planning for future developments.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Figures/model.pdf} \caption{Illustration of the whole framework of UrbanDiT, including four key components: a) Unifying different urban spatio-temporal data types; b) The diffusion pipeline of our UrbanDiT; c) Different masking strategies to specify different tasks; d) Unified prompt learning with data-driven and task-specific prompts to enhance the denoising process. } \label{fig:model}
elements.sent-2411-079: Figure~\ref{fig:model illustrates the overall framework of our proposed UrbanDiT, which is based on diffusion transformers.}
elements.sent-2411-080: This framework seamlessly integrates various data types and tasks into a cohesive model.
elements.sent-2411-082: We convert data, characterized by a three-dimensional structure (2D spatial and 1D temporal dimensions), into a unified sequential format.
elements.sent-2411-083: For the temporal dimension, we employ patching techniques commonly used in foundational models for time series~\citep{nie2022time .}
elements.sent-2411-084: For grid-based data, we apply 2D patching methods, which are widely utilized in image processing, to organize the data.
elements.sent-2411-085: This allows us to rearrange the three-dimensional grid data into a one-dimensional sequential format.
elements.sent-2411-086: For graph-based data, we use Graph Convolutional Networks (GCN)~\citep{zhang2019graph to process each node and integrate it with the temporal dimension to reshape the data into a one-dimensional format as well.}
elements.sent-2411-087: More details of data unification can be found in Appendix~\ref{append:token.}
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=0.99\linewidth]{Figures/mask_task.pdf} \caption{Masking strategies to specify various urban spatio-temporal tasks.} \label{fig:mask_task}
elements.sent-2411-088: To adapt to various tasks, we employ a unified masking strategy.
elements.sent-2411-089: As illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:mask_task , these tasks can be framed as reconstructing missing parts of the data, with distinct masking strategies tailored to each task.}
elements.sent-2411-090: For Forward Prediction, we mask future time steps while utilizing past and present data points to predict the missing values.
elements.sent-2411-091: Conversely, for Backward Prediction, we mask past time steps to estimate historical values based on current and future observations.
elements.sent-2411-092: In the case of temporal interpolation tasks, we apply masks to specific time points within a continuous series, allowing the model to fill in these gaps.
elements.sent-2411-093: For spatio-temporal imputation, we randomly mask missing values across both spatial and temporal dimensions, enabling the model to leverage surrounding context for accurate estimations.
elements.sent-2411-094: Finally, in spatial extrapolation tasks, we mask areas outside the observed spatial domain to predict values for unobserved regions based on existing spatial patterns.
elements.sent-2411-095: Consequently, the input of the denoising network $X^t$ is represented as the concatenation of noise features and unmasked spatio-temporal data (conditional observations): \begin{equation}X^t = X^t \ast (1-M) + X^0 \ast M \nonumber\end{equation} where $X^t$ denotes the noise features, $M$ is the mask that controls the availability of values for downstream tasks, and $X^0$ represents the clean values of the spatio-temporal data.
elements.sent-2411-096: In this way, we can modulate different masks $M$ to facilitate various urban spatio-temporal applications.
elements.sent-2411-098: We first apply temporal patching to process time series data at each spatial location, represented as \(X^{N \times T' \times D = Conv(X^{N \times T \times D )\), where \(T' = \frac{T {p_t}\) and \(p_t\) is the temporal patch size.}}}
elements.sent-2411-099: Next, for grid-based data, we implement 2D spatial patching, resulting in \(X_p = Conv_{2D (X^{H \times W \times T' \times D )\), where \(X_p \in \mathbb{R ^{L \times D , L=\frac{H\times W \times T {p_s\times p_s \times p_t}\).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-100: In this way, we effectively reorganize the data into a format that is conducive to transformer architectures.. Spatio-Temporal Transformer Block.
elements.sent-2411-101: The overall model is composed of multiple spatio-temporal transformer blocks.
elements.sent-2411-102: Each block features both temporal multi-head attention and spatial multi-head attention, with spatial and temporal attention mechanisms operating independently.
elements.sent-2411-103: This design choice is made to enhance computational efficiency, as the complexity of attention scales with the square of the sequence length.. Diffusion Transformer.
elements.sent-2411-104: We adopt the diffusion transformer model, which integrates a denoising network designed to process complex inputs effectively.
elements.sent-2411-105: The inputs to the denoising network consist of three key components: the noisy spatio-temporal data, the timestep, and the prompt.
elements.sent-2411-106: For the timestep $t$, we utilize them for layer normalization following previous practices~\citep{peebles2023scalable,luvdt , which helps stabilize and standardize the input features at each timestep.}
elements.sent-2411-107: The prompt, which provides contextual information or guidance for the model, is concatenated with the input data to enhance the model’s understanding of the data and task at hand.
elements.sent-2411-108: This concatenation is straightforward due to the transformer's capability to manage variable sequence lengths, providing flexibility in processing diverse inputs.
elements.sent-2411-109: By incorporating these elements, the diffusion transformer model effectively learns to denoise and generate robust desired results in spatio-temporal contexts.
elements.sent-2411-110: We propose a unified prompt learning framework to enhance the diffusion transformers' universality across various data types and tasks.
elements.sent-2411-111: The data-driven prompt is crucial for training a unified model with multiple and diverse datasets, as such datasets often exhibit significant variations in patterns and distributions.
elements.sent-2411-112: In this context, the prompt acts as a guiding mechanism, helping the model to effectively navigate these differences and generate accurate results.
elements.sent-2411-113: Similar to retrieval-augmented generation, prompts retrieve the most relevant information, enhancing the model's ability to contextualize and interpret spatio-temporal data.
elements.sent-2411-114: By aligning the model's learning process with the specific characteristics of various spatio-temporal patterns, data prompts ensure that UrbanDiT can adaptively respond to a wide range of urban spatio-temporal scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-115: To achieve this goal, we employ memory networks, specifically utilizing three memory pools designed to capture the time-domain, frequency-domain and spatial patterns of spatio-temporal dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-116: For different input data, the prompt network retrieves prompts from these memory pools based on the respective time-domain, frequency-domain, and spatial patterns.
elements.sent-2411-117: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:prompt , each memory pool is structured as a key-value store \((K_t, V_t) = \{(k_t^1, v_t^1), ..., (k_t^N, v_t^N)\}, \ (K_f, V_f) = \{(k_f^1, v_f^1), ..., (k_f^N, v_f^N)\}, \ (K_s, V_s) = \{(k_s^1, v_s^1), ..., (k_s^N, v_s^N)\}\), where both keys and values are learnable embeddings and randomly initialized.}

elements.sent-2411-124: The training process alternates between multiple datasets and tasks.
elements.sent-2411-125: In each iteration, we randomly select a dataset and a corresponding task to perform gradient descent training.
elements.sent-2411-126: This approach enhances the model's robustness by exposing it to diverse scenarios and helps prevent overfitting by ensuring the model learns from a wide range of inputs and objectives.

elements.sent-2411-171: A key strength of foundation models is their ability to generalize easily.
elements.sent-2411-172: Therefore, we perform experiments in both few-shot and zero-shot scenarios, testing its adaptability to new datasets with little or no additional training.
elements.sent-2411-173: In the few-shot scenario, we train UrbanDiT on a small portion of the target dataset—specifically using only 5\% and 10\% of the available data—and then evaluate its performance on the corresponding test set.
elements.sent-2411-174: This setup challenges the model to generalize well from sparse data.
elements.sent-2411-175: In the zero-shot scenario, no data from the target dataset is provided for training.
elements.sent-2411-176: Instead, we directly evaluate UrbanDiT’s performance on the target dataset, relying solely on its pretrained knowledge to handle unseen data without any fine-tuning.

elements.sent-2411-178: In the few-shot setting (with 5\% and 1\% of the training data), UrbanDiT consistently outperforms the baselines, showcasing its strong ability to learn from minimal data.
elements.sent-2411-179: Even more striking, in the zero-shot scenario, UrbanDiT exhibits exceptional inference capabilities, surpassing nearly all baseline models that had access to training data.
elements.sent-2411-180: This highlights its exceptional generalization ability without fine-tuning, reinforcing its effectiveness as an open-world foundation model.

elements.sent-2411-181: Unified prompt learning is a key design in UrbanDiT.
elements.sent-2411-182: To investigate the contribution of each prompt to the final performance, we conduct ablation studies by systematically removing each type of prompt.
elements.sent-2411-183: Specifically, we identify four types of prompts: $F$ for frequency-domain prompt, $T$ for time-domain prompt, $S$ for spatial prompt, and $M$ for task-specific prompt.
elements.sent-2411-184: We denote the removal of a prompt as w/o $\{F, T, S, M\}$ and indicate the absence of any prompt as w/o $P$.
elements.sent-2411-185: Figure~\ref{fig:ablation_prompt presents the results of ablation studies.}
elements.sent-2411-186: The findings reveal that removing any single prompt significantly degrades the model's performance.
elements.sent-2411-187: In the absence of prompt design altogether, the model exhibits the poorest performance.
elements.sent-2411-188: Among the four types of prompts, the removal of the frequency-domain prompt has the most pronounced negative impact on the overall performance.
elements.sent-2411-190: We further investigate the effect of inference steps on the performance of diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-191: The number of inference steps is a critical factor in balancing the model’s accuracy and efficiency.

elements.sent-2411-193: Notably, we observe that around 20 inference steps provide the optimal balance between computational efficiency and model performance for both datasets.
elements.sent-2411-194: By setting the diffusion steps to 500 and the inference steps to 20, we achieve a 25x improvement in efficiency compared to the original DDPM~\citep{ho2020denoising , without sacrificing accuracy.}
elements.sent-2411-195: This information is valuable for practitioners to train and fine-tune the foundation model effectively.

elements.sent-2411-197: As observed, all three models demonstrate improved performance as the data size increases.
elements.sent-2411-198: However, when the dataset size increases from 0.8 to 1, the large model, UrbanDiT-L, shows a notably steeper improvement (with a slope of 0.011), compared to the medium (slope of 0.0015) and small models (slope of 0.0019).
elements.sent-2411-199: This pronounced scaling effect for the large model indicates its potential to further enhance performance as more data becomes available.
elements.sent-2411-200: These results highlight the promising scalability of UrbanDiT-L, suggesting that it can effectively handle larger datasets and achieve even better outcomes with increased data size.
elements.sent-2411-201: In this paper, we present UrbanDiT, an open-world foundation model built on a diffusion transformers and a unified prompt learning framework.
elements.sent-2411-202: UrbanDiT enables seamless adaptation to a wide range of urban spatio-temporal tasks across diverse datasets from urban environments.
elements.sent-2411-203: Our extensive experiments highlight the model's exceptional potential in advancing the field of urban spatio-temporal modeling.
elements.sent-2411-204: We believe this work not only pushes the boundaries of urban spatio-temporal modeling but also serves as an inspire future research in the rapidly evolving field of foundation models.. \bibliographystyle{iclr2025_conference .}

elements.sent-2411-205: We provide a detailed overview of the datasets utilized in our study to support future research in the field of urban spatio-temporal modeling.
elements.sent-2411-206: The datasets are categorized into two distinct types: grid-based and graph-based spatio-temporal data.
elements.sent-2411-207: Each type of data reflects different spatial organizations and dynamics, enabling a comprehensive evaluation of model performance across varied urban scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-208: Grid-based data represent spatial information in a structured, uniform grid layout, where each grid cell corresponds to a specific geographical area.
elements.sent-2411-209: Table~\ref{tbl:append_data_grid outlines the essential details and statistics for the grid-based datasets, including spatial resolution, temporal resolution, temporal period, and the size of each dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-210: Graph-based data, on the other hand, capture urban spatial relationships through a network of nodes and edges, where nodes typically represent points of interest (e.g., intersections or key locations), and edges represent the connections between them (e.g., roads or transit lines).
elements.sent-2411-211: This type of data is well-suited for modeling scenarios that involve irregular spatial structures, such as transportation networks.
elements.sent-2411-212: Table~\ref{tbl:append_data_graph provides a comprehensive summary of the graph-based datasets, including information on the number of nodes, edges, temporal resolution, temporal period, and dataset size.}
elements.sent-2411-213: We provide a detailed description of the data unification process for both grid-based and graph-based spatio-temporal data.
elements.sent-2411-214: The key goal is to transform the data into a unified sequential format suitable for the transformer’s input.
elements.sent-2411-215: Grid-based data is structured in a uniform grid layout, typically represented in a three-dimensional form $X_{\text{grid \in \mathbb{R ^{ T \times H \times W}$ with two spatial dimensions (height $H$ and width $W$) and one temporal dimension $T$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-216: To process this data, we utilize 3D Convolutional Neural Networks (3D CNN), which are widely used for capturing both spatial and temporal dependencies in spatio-temporal tasks.
elements.sent-2411-217: The process is formulated as follows: \begin{equation}X' = Conv3D(X_{grid}, kernel size = (p_t, p_s, p_s)) \nonumber X_p = Reshape(X',[N]) \nonumber\end{equation} where $N=\frac{T {p_t}\times \frac{H {p_s}\times \frac{W {p_s}$ represents the total number of spatio-temporal partitions, effectively converting the data into a one-dimensional sequence for further processing by the transformer model.}}}
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}X' = Conv3D(X_{grid}, kernel size = (p_t, p_s, p_s)) \nonumber X_p = Reshape(X',[N]) \nonumber\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-218: Graph-based data is inherently non-Euclidean, capturing relationships between urban entities (e.g., streets and intersections).
elements.sent-2411-219: The spatial dimension is represented by a graph structure with nodes and edges, and the temporal dimension is still captured as a time series at each node.
elements.sent-2411-220: The graph-based data can be represented as a tensor $X_{\text{graph \in \mathbb{R ^{N \times T}$, where $N$ is the number of nodes in the graph, and $T$ is the number of time steps.}}}
elements.sent-2411-221: To handle the temporal dimension, we first apply a 1D convolutional network (1D CNN) along the time axis to capture local temporal dependencies.
elements.sent-2411-222: Next, to capture spatial relationships, we apply a Graph Convolutional Network (GCN)~\citep{zhang2019graph on the graph structure.}
elements.sent-2411-223: For each temporal patch, the GCN aggregates information from neighboring nodes using the graph’s adjacency matrix $A \in \mathbb{R ^{N \times N}$.}
elements.sent-2411-224: Finally, we reshape the graph-based data into a sequential format.

elements.sent-2411-230: In our work, we employ four distinct approaches to compute features in the frequency domain, depending on the configuration of the Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) and thresholding mechanisms:.
elements.sent-2411-231: Without FFT Threshold: we directly compute the FFT of the input tensor.
elements.sent-2411-232: The tensor is permuted along the appropriate dimensions, and the real and imaginary components of the FFT are concatenated along the last dimension.
elements.sent-2411-233: This results in a frequency domain representation of the data.
elements.sent-2411-234: It is formulated as follows: \begin{equation}X_{FFT} = FFT(X), \nonumber X_{freq} = \left[ \Re(X_{FFT}), \Im(X_{FFT}) \right], \nonumber\end{equation} where $\Re(X_{\text{FFT )$ represents the real part of the FFT, and $\Im(X_{\text{FFT )$ represents the imaginary part.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-235: Basic FFT Threshold: we apply a basic threshold technique by computing the amplitude of the FFT and creating a binary mask.
elements.sent-2411-236: The mask retains frequency components whose amplitude is greater than the mean amplitude, filtering out low-frequency noise and preserving significant frequency components.

elements.sent-2411-246: HA: History Average is a forecasting method that predicts future values by calculating the mean of historical data from the same time periods.
elements.sent-2411-247: MIM~\citep{wang2019memory : This model utilizes the difference in data between consecutive recurring states to address non-stationary characteristics.}
elements.sent-2411-248: By stacking multiple MIM blocks, it can capture higher-order non-stationarity in the data.
elements.sent-2411-249: MAU~\citep{chang2021mau : The Motion-aware Unit extends the temporal scope of prediction units to seize correlations in motion between frames.}
elements.sent-2411-250: It encompasses an attention mechanism and a fusion mechanism, which are integral to video prediction tasks.
elements.sent-2411-251: SimVP~\citep{gao2022simvp : A simple yet effective video prediction model is entirely based on convolutional neural networks and employs MSE loss as its performance metric, providing a reliable benchmark for comparative studies in video prediction.}
elements.sent-2411-252: TAU~\citep{tan2023temporal : The Temporal Attention Module breaks down temporal attention into two parts: within-frame and between-frames, and employs differential divergence regularization to manage variations across frames.}
elements.sent-2411-253: STResNet~\citep{zhang2017deep : STResNet employs residual neural networks to detect proximity, periodicity, and trends in the temporal data.}
elements.sent-2411-254: ACFM~\citep{liu2018attentive : The Attentive Crowd Flow Machine model forecasts crowd movements by using an attention mechanism to dynamically integrate sequential and cyclical patterns.}
elements.sent-2411-255: STGSP~\citep{zhao2022st : This model highlights the significance of global and positional temporal data for spatio-temporal forecasting.}
elements.sent-2411-256: It incorporates a semantic flow encoder to capture temporal position cues and an attention mechanism to handle multi-scale temporal interactions.
elements.sent-2411-257: MC-STL~\citep{zhang2023mask : MC-STL utilizes mask-enhanced contrastive learning to efficiently identify spatio-temporal relationships.}
elements.sent-2411-258: STNorm~\citep{deng2021st : It introduces two distinct normalization modules: spatial normalization for handling high-frequency elements and temporal normalization for managing local components.}
elements.sent-2411-259: STID~\citep{shao2022spatial : This MLP-based spatio-temporal forecasting model discerns subtleties within the spatial and temporal axes, showcasing its design's efficiency and efficacy.}
elements.sent-2411-260: PromptST~\citep{zhang2023promptst : An advanced pre-training and prompt-tuning methodology tailored for spatio-temporal forecasting.}
elements.sent-2411-261: UniST~\citep{yuan2024unist : A versatile urban spatio-temporal prediction model that uses grid-based data.}
elements.sent-2411-262: It employs various spatio-temporal masking techniques for pre-training and fine-tuning with spatio-temporal knowledge-based prompts.
elements.sent-2411-263: STGCN~\citep{yu2018spatio : The Spatio-Temporal Graph Convolutional Network is a deep learning architecture for predicting traffic patterns, harnessing both spatial and temporal correlations.}
elements.sent-2411-264: It integrates graph convolutional operations with convolutional sequence learning to capture multi-scale dynamics within traffic networks.
elements.sent-2411-265: GWN~\citep{wu2019graph : Graph WaveNet is a technique crafted to overcome the shortcomings of current spatial-temporal graph modeling methods.}
elements.sent-2411-266: It introduces a self-adjusting adjacency matrix and utilizes stacked dilated causal convolutions to efficiently capture temporal relationships.
elements.sent-2411-267: MTGNN~\citep{wu2020connecting : MTGNN is a framework tailored for multivariate time series analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-268: It autonomously identifies directional relationships between variables via a graph learning component and incorporates additional information such as variable attributes.
elements.sent-2411-269: GTS~\citep{shang2021discrete : GTS is an approach that concurrently learns the topology of a graph alongside a Graph Neural Network (GNN) for predicting multiple time series.}
elements.sent-2411-270: It models the graph structure using a neural network, allowing for the generation of distinct graph samples, and aims to optimize the average performance across the distribution of graphs.
elements.sent-2411-271: DCRNN~\citep{li2018diffusion : The Diffusion Convolutional Recurrent Neural Network is a deep learning framework for spatiotemporal prediction.}
elements.sent-2411-272: It treats traffic flow as a diffusion phenomenon on a directed graph, securing spatial interdependencies via two-way random walks and temporal interdependencies through an encoder-decoder setup with scheduled sampling.
elements.sent-2411-273: STEP~\citep{shao2022pre :Spatial-temporal Graph Neural Network Enhanced by Pre-training is a framework that uses a pre-trained model to enhance spatial-temporal graph neural networks for better forecasting of multivariate time series data.}
elements.sent-2411-274: AGCRN~\citep{bai2020adaptive : The AGCRN framework improves upon Graph Convolutional Networks by incorporating two adaptive components: Node Adaptive Parameter Learning and Data Adaptive Graph Generation.}
elements.sent-2411-275: This approach effectively captures nuanced spatial and temporal relationships within traffic data, functioning independently of pre-set graph structures.
elements.sent-2411-276: PatchTST~\citep{nie2022time : It employs patching and self-supervised learning techniques for forecasting multivariate time series.}
elements.sent-2411-277: By dividing the time series into segments, it captures long-term dependencies and analyzes each data channel separately using a unified network architecture.
elements.sent-2411-278: Time-LLM~\citep{jin2023time : TIME-LLM represents an advanced approach in applying large-scale language models to time series prediction.}
elements.sent-2411-279: It employs a reprogramming strategy that adapts LLMs for forecasting tasks without altering the underlying language model architecture.
elements.sent-2411-280: CSDI~\citep{tashiro2021csdi : CSDI is explicitly trained for imputation and can exploit correlations between observed values, leading to significant improvements in performance over existing probabilistic imputation methods.}
elements.sent-2411-281: Imputeformer~\citep{nie2024imputeformer : It introduces a low-rank inductive bias into the Transformer framework to balance strong inductive priors with high model expressivity, making it suitable for a wide range of imputation tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-282: Grin~\citep{cini2022filling : GRIN introduces a novel graph neural network architecture designed to reconstruct missing data in different channels of a multivariate time series, outperforming state-of-the-art methods in imputation tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-283: BriTS~\citep{cao2018brits : BRITS is a method for imputing missing values in time series data, utilizing a bidirectional recurrent neural network (RNN) without imposing assumptions on the data's underlying dynamics.}
elements.sent-2411-284: It is worth noting that the baselines, including UniST~\citep{yuan2024unist and PatchTST~\citep{nie2022time , can also be trained using multiple datasets.}}
elements.sent-2411-285: In our comparison experiments, we train these models in a unified manner using the same diverse datasets to ensure a fair comparison.
elements.sent-2411-286: This approach ensures that the performance gains of UrbanDiT are not merely due to dataset diversity, but reflect the model's true advantage.

elements.sent-2411-299: In this work, we introduce UrbanDiT, a foundation model for open-world urban spatio-temporal learning that successfully scales up diffusion transformers in this field.
elements.sent-2411-300: UrbanDiT pioneers a unified model that integrates diverse data sources and types while learning universal spatio-temporal patterns across different cities and scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-301: UrbanDiT offers three advantages: 1) It unifies diverse data types, such as grid-based and graph-based data, into a sequential format; 2) With task-specific prompts, it supports a wide range of tasks, including bi-directional spatio-temporal prediction, temporal interpolation, spatial extrapolation, and spatio-temporal imputation; and 3) It generalizes effectively to open-world scenarios, with its powerful zero-shot capabilities outperforming nearly all baselines with training data.
elements.sent-2411-302: Code and datasets are publicly available at \url{https://github.com/tsinghua-fib-lab/UrbanDiT .}
elements.sent-2411-303: The data from different cities are usually with unique layouts, infrastructures, and planning strategies.
elements.sent-2411-304: Effectively modeling their spatio-temporal dynamics is crucial for optimizing urban services and understanding how cities function.

elements.sent-2411-305: Urban spatio-temporal data is usually defined by varying spatio-temporal resolutions and complex interactions among entities.
elements.sent-2411-306: Building a foundation model requires a scalable architecture capable of accommodating these complexities.
elements.sent-2411-307: It offers three appealing benefits: 1) It unifies diverse data types into a sequential format, allowing it to capture spatio-temporal patterns across various cities and domains.
elements.sent-2411-308: To build UrbanDiT, we first unify different input data by converting it into the sequential format.
elements.sent-2411-309: We transformer blocks as the denoising network, which are equipped with both temporal and spatial attention modules.
elements.sent-2411-310: To integrate diverse data types and tasks, we propose a unified prompt learning framework.
elements.sent-2411-311: It maintains memory pools to capture learned spatio-temporal patterns and generate data-driven prompts, while also create task-specific prompts for various spatio-temporal tasks.
elements.sent-2411-312: To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to explore a foundation model for general-purpose urban spatio-temporal learning, integrating diverse data types and multiple urabn tasks within a single unified model.

elements.sent-2411-351: Question: Do the main claims made in the abstract and introduction accurately reflect the paper's contributions and scope?.
elements.sent-2411-353: The answer NA means that the abstract and introduction do not include the claims made in the paper.
elements.sent-2411-354: The abstract and/or introduction should clearly state the claims made, including the contributions made in the paper and important assumptions and limitations.
elements.sent-2411-355: A No or NA answer to this question will not be perceived well by the reviewers.
elements.sent-2411-356: The claims made should match theoretical and experimental results, and reflect how much the results can be expected to generalize to other settings.
elements.sent-2411-357: It is fine to include aspirational goals as motivation as long as it is clear that these goals are not attained by the paper.. \end{itemize.}
elements.sent-2411-358: Question: Does the paper discuss the limitations of the work performed by the authors?.
elements.sent-2411-360: The answer NA means that the paper has no limitation while the answer No means that the paper has limitations, but those are not discussed in the paper.
elements.sent-2411-361: The authors are encouraged to create a separate "Limitations" section in their paper.
elements.sent-2411-362: The paper should point out any strong assumptions and how robust the results are to violations of these assumptions (e.g., independence assumptions, noiseless settings, model well-specification, asymptotic approximations only holding locally).
elements.sent-2411-363: The authors should reflect on how these assumptions might be violated in practice and what the implications would be.
elements.sent-2411-364: The authors should reflect on the scope of the claims made, e.g., if the approach was only tested on a few datasets or with a few runs.
elements.sent-2411-365: In general, empirical results often depend on implicit assumptions, which should be articulated.
elements.sent-2411-366: The authors should reflect on the factors that influence the performance of the approach.
elements.sent-2411-367: For example, a facial recognition algorithm may perform poorly when image resolution is low or images are taken in low lighting.
elements.sent-2411-368: Or a speech-to-text system might not be used reliably to provide closed captions for online lectures because it fails to handle technical jargon.
elements.sent-2411-369: The authors should discuss the computational efficiency of the proposed algorithms and how they scale with dataset size.
elements.sent-2411-370: If applicable, the authors should discuss possible limitations of their approach to address problems of privacy and fairness.
elements.sent-2411-371: While the authors might fear that complete honesty about limitations might be used by reviewers as grounds for rejection, a worse outcome might be that reviewers discover limitations that aren't acknowledged in the paper.
elements.sent-2411-372: The authors should use their best judgment and recognize that individual actions in favor of transparency play an important role in developing norms that preserve the integrity of the community.
elements.sent-2411-373: Reviewers will be specifically instructed to not penalize honesty concerning limitations.. \end{itemize.}

elements.sent-2411-376: The answer NA means that the paper does not include theoretical results.
elements.sent-2411-377: All the theorems, formulas, and proofs in the paper should be numbered and cross-referenced.

elements.sent-2411-379: The proofs can either appear in the main paper or the supplemental material, but if they appear in the supplemental material, the authors are encouraged to provide a short proof sketch to provide intuition.

elements.sent-2411-381: Theorems and Lemmas that the proof relies upon should be properly referenced.. \end{itemize.}
elements.sent-2411-382: Question: Does the paper fully disclose all the information needed to reproduce the main experimental results of the paper to the extent that it affects the main claims and/or conclusions of the paper (regardless of whether the code and data are provided or not)?.

elements.sent-2411-385: The answer NA means that the paper does not include experiments.

elements.sent-2411-387: If the contribution is a dataset and/or model, the authors should describe the steps taken to make their results reproducible or verifiable.

elements.sent-2411-389: For example, if the contribution is a novel architecture, describing the architecture fully might suffice, or if the contribution is a specific model and empirical evaluation, it may be necessary to either make it possible for others to replicate the model with the same dataset, or provide access to the model.. elements.sent-2411-390: While NeurIPS does not require releasing code, the conference does require all submissions to provide some reasonable avenue for reproducibility, which may depend on the nature of the contribution.. For example \begin{enumerate.}
elements.sent-2411-391: If the contribution is primarily a new algorithm, the paper should make it clear how to reproduce that algorithm.
